#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Open action: navigate the workspace browser session to a URL, or attach
# view-only when invoked with no URL (action invoke and keybindings cannot
# pass arguments in herdr 0.7.x, so URL-less is the primary invoke path).
set -uo pipefail
cd "${HERDR_PLUGIN_ROOT:-$(dirname "$0")/..}" || exit 1
. scripts/lib.sh

require_agent_browser

url="${1:-${HERDR_PLUGIN_CLICKED_URL:-}}"
session="$(session_name)"

if [ -n "$url" ]; then
  if ! validate_url "$url"; then
    echo "herdr-browser: refusing URL (must start with http:// or https://): $url" >&2
    exit 2
  fi
  # If this call spawns the session daemon, let it self-reap after 30 min
  # idle instead of living forever (no-op for daemons agents already own).
  export AGENT_BROWSER_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS="${AGENT_BROWSER_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS:-1800000}"
  if ! agent-browser --session "$session" open "$url" >/dev/null; then
    echo "herdr-browser: agent-browser failed to open $url" >&2
    exit 3
  fi
fi

# Serialize concurrent opens: two invokes racing past the liveness check
# would each open a pane (herdr pane open is not idempotent). mkdir is the
# portable atomic lock; after ~5s of waiting the holder is presumed dead and
# the lock is stolen — a duplicate pane beats an open action that never acts.
lock="$(state_dir)/open-lock-${HERDR_WORKSPACE_ID:-default}"
tries=0
until mkdir "$lock" 2>/dev/null; do
  tries=$((tries + 1))
  if [ "$tries" -gt "${HERDR_BROWSER_LOCK_TRIES:-50}" ]; then
    rm -rf "$lock"
  fi
  sleep 0.1
done
trap 'rmdir "$lock" 2>/dev/null' EXIT

pidfile="$(pane_id_file)"
existing=""
[ -f "$pidfile" ] && existing="$(cat "$pidfile")"

if pane_alive "$existing"; then
  "$HERDR" plugin pane focus "$existing" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
else
  out="$("$HERDR" plugin pane open --plugin "$PLUGIN_ID" --entrypoint view \
    --placement split --direction right --focus)" || {
    echo "herdr-browser: failed to open pane" >&2
    exit 4
  }
  pane_id="$(parse_pane_id "$out")"
  if [ -n "$pane_id" ]; then
    printf '%s\n' "$pane_id" > "$pidfile"
  else
    echo "herdr-browser: warning: could not parse pane id from pane-open output" >&2
    rm -f "$pidfile"
  fi
fi

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Close action: close this workspace's browser panes first (so the renderers
# exit cleanly), then end this plugin's browser session. Never touches other
# workspaces or other sessions.
set -uo pipefail
cd "${HERDR_PLUGIN_ROOT:-$(dirname "$0")/..}" || exit 1
. scripts/lib.sh

session="$(session_name)"
closed=0

close_pane() {
  [ -n "$1" ] || return 0
  if "$HERDR" pane close "$1" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    closed=$((closed + 1))
  fi
  return 0
}

# Tracked panes: the view pane (open.sh) and any browse panes (browse.sh).
# Close unconditionally — a liveness pre-check that fails would strand a live
# pane, while closing an already-dead id is harmless.
for f in "$(pane_id_file)" "$(browse_ids_file)"; do
  if [ -f "$f" ]; then

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Close action: close this workspace's browser panes first (so the renderers
# exit cleanly), then end this plugin's browser session. Never touches other
# workspaces or other sessions.
set -uo pipefail
cd "${HERDR_PLUGIN_ROOT:-$(dirname "$0")/..}" || exit 1
. scripts/lib.sh

session="$(session_name)"
closed=0

close_pane() {
  [ -n "$1" ] || return 0
  if "$HERDR" pane close "$1" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    closed=$((closed + 1))
  fi
  return 0
}

# Tracked panes: the view pane (open.sh) and any browse panes (browse.sh).
# Close unconditionally — a liveness pre-check that fails would strand a live
# pane, while closing an already-dead id is harmless.
for f in "$(pane_id_file)" "$(browse_ids_file)"; do
  if [ -f "$f" ]; then
    while IFS= read -r id; do close_pane "$id"; done < "$f"
    rm -f "$f"

done

fi

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Browse action: open an interactive carbonyl browser pane. Unlike the viewer
# pane, this is a personal browser — its own Chromium, no shared agent session.
set -uo pipefail
cd "${HERDR_PLUGIN_ROOT:-$(dirname "$0")/..}" || exit 1
. scripts/lib.sh

url="${1:-${HERDR_PLUGIN_CLICKED_URL:-}}"
if [ -n "$url" ] && ! validate_url "$url"; then
  echo "herdr-browser: refusing URL (must start with http:// or https://): $url" >&2
  exit 2
fi

args=(plugin pane open --plugin "$PLUGIN_ID" --entrypoint browse
  --placement zoomed --focus)
if [ -n "$url" ]; then
  args+=(--env "HERDR_BROWSE_URL=$url")
fi
out="$("$HERDR" "${args[@]}")" || {
  echo "herdr-browser: failed to open browse pane" >&2
  exit 4
}
# Record the pane so the close action can find it later.
pane_id="$(parse_pane_id "$out")"
if [ -n "$pane_id" ]; then
  printf '%s\n' "$pane_id" >> "$(browse_ids_file)"
else
  echo "herdr-browser: warning: could not parse pane id from pane-open output" >&2
fi

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Pane entrypoint: run the renderer. Never exit instantly on a missing
# dependency — an exiting pane process closes the pane before the user can
# read the error.
cd "${HERDR_PLUGIN_ROOT:-$(dirname "$0")/..}" || exit 1

if ! command -v node >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "herdr-browser: node not found on PATH (need Node.js >= 20)."
  echo "Install Node.js, then reopen this pane."
  sleep 600
  exit 1
fi

# Resolve the session once through the shared bash helper so the launcher and
# the renderer can never derive different names.
. scripts/lib.sh
HERDR_BROWSER_SESSION="$(session_name)"
export HERDR_BROWSER_SESSION

exec node bin/renderer.mjs

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Interactive browser pane: carbonyl renders Chromium straight into the
# terminal with full mouse/keyboard support. Prompts for a URL when none was
# passed, so a bare keybinding acts as an address bar.
cd "${HERDR_PLUGIN_ROOT:-$(dirname "$0")/..}" || exit 1

if ! command -v carbonyl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "herdr-browser: carbonyl not found."
  echo "Install it with: npm install -g carbonyl"
  echo "Then reopen this pane."
  sleep 600
  exit 1
fi

url="${HERDR_BROWSE_URL:-}"
if [ -z "$url" ]; then
  printf 'URL: '
  IFS= read -r url
  if [ -z "$url" ]; then
    exit 0
  fi
  case "$url" in
    http://*|https://*) ;;
    *) url="https://$url" ;;
  esac
fi

zoom="${HERDR_BROWSE_ZOOM:-}"
if [ -z "$zoom" ] && [ -n "${HERDR_PLUGIN_CONFIG_DIR:-}" ] && [ -f "${HERDR_PLUGIN_CONFIG_DIR}/zoom" ]; then
  zoom="$(head -n1 "${HERDR_PLUGIN_CONFIG_DIR}/zoom" | tr -cd '0-9')"
fi

exec carbonyl --zoom="${zoom:-100}" "$url"

   - `redrawAll`, `cleanup` — cleanup: selfCreated → spawnSync close; shot files removed; raw mode restore; escape sequence written. Untested. cleanup's selfCreated close is an important ownership behavior: quit-pane-closes-session only if we created it. Testable: instantiate, set selfCreated, point state dir to tmp, monkeypatch... cleanup calls spawnSync('agent-browser', ...) directly — the binary name is hardcoded 'agent-browser' in cleanup (not using this.browser's bin). Hmm, cleanup uses spawnSync('agent-browser', ...) — that resolves via PATH. In tests, PATH manipulation works for spawnSync too since it's a fresh exec. Or monkeypatch child_process? The module imports spawnSync directly, so test must control PATH. Feasible: create Renderer with stub dir PATH... but constructor runs spawnSync('sh', ['-c', 'command -v chafa']) and deriveSession may run cksum. PATH with stub agent-browser plus /usr/bin works.
   - `run()` — the poll loop is infinite; integration-ish test would spawn the renderer as a child process with a stub agent-browser and assert on stdout. The task hints at this. The main-module guard uses `import.meta.url === pathToFileURL(process.argv[1]).href` — spawnable via `node bin/renderer.mjs` with env HERDR_BROWSER_SESSION, HERDR_PLUGIN_STATE_DIR, PATH with stub agent-browser, no TTY (stdin not TTY → setupInput returns early; probeKitty resolves '' since not TTY → mode = symbols if chafa available else text; chafa probe via PATH — control it). Then the loop ticks every >= 250ms (HERDR_BROWSER_INTERVAL_MS=250). Assert stdout contains 'waiting for session' banner when session absent; then flip stub to say session exists and assert URL appears. Kill with SIGTERM. This is very feasible with node:test and would cover tick's attach transition, header, and the passive invariant end-to-end. High value recommendation.

6. `pollDelay` — tested boundaries 9/10, 29/30? Test checks 0, 9, 10, 30, 60, 10000. 29 and 59 not checked but 30 and 60 are the boundary starts; fine. Good coverage.

7. `mapClickToPage` — tested: one mapping, below-image null, above-image null (row 2 < imageTopRow → unitY negative → null). Untested:
   - pngW or pngH = 0 → null (guard `!pngW || !pngH`).
   - imageRows <= 0 → null.
   - scale NaN/negative guard `!(scale > 0)` — when cols = 0? cols comes from terminal; not reachable via geom in test... cols: 0 → scale = min(0, ...) = 0 → null. Testable.
   - right-edge beyond drawn width → null (unitX > pngW * scale). Untested: letterbox on the right side (portrait image in wide terminal). The tested letterbox is only bottom (unitY). Right-letterbox click → null is untested.
   - Rounding behavior: Math.round at fractional boundaries.
   - Click at exact 1,1 of image (col=1, row=imageTopRow) → unitX=0.5, unitY=1 → small x,y. Fine.
   - Note the comment about aspect: chafa preserves pixel aspect with 1:2 cell ratio. The math: unitY = (row - imageTopRow)*2 + 1. Row index from SGR is 1-based; imageTopRow=3. At row=3: unitY = 1. OK.

8. `parseSgrMouse` — tested press/release/non-mouse. Untested: scroll wheel buttons (button 64/65), modifier bits (button with modifiers, e.g. 4 = shift). The renderer only acts on button === 0, so modifier clicks (shift+click → button 4) are ignored — is that intended? Maybe; but parse test for button>0 is trivial. Also malformed sequences like '\x1b[<0;40M' → null. Multiple sequences in one chunk — regex only captures the first! `parseSgrMouse` uses `.exec` on the string and onInput processes only the first match; a chunk containing two mouse reports (fast clicking) drops the second. That's a behavior note, not necessarily a bug (poll will still refresh). Worth noting as a gap/observation: onInput handles a single mouse event per chunk; batched input loses events. Similarly key input: `switch (s)` on the whole chunk — if the terminal delivers 'jj' in one chunk, neither scroll happens (no case matches 'jj'). Minor UX robustness gap, worth mentioning as observation (not a test-only issue). Actually for promptInput they loop over chars — but for the main switch they don't. Real behavioral asymmetry. It's a production observation; as testing reviewer I can note missing tests pin this behavior either way.

9. `sanitizeText` — tested: OSC with BEL, kitty sequence, tab→space, \r\n stripped, DEL, C1 (\u009b), unicode preserved, non-string number coerced. Untested: \u0080-\u009a and \u009c-\u009f range edges (only \u009b tested); high unicode beyond BMP; the regex covers \u007f-\u009f. Edge anchors: \u001f and \u0020 boundary (\u0020 space must survive — implicitly tested by 'plain — unicode ✓ stays' containing spaces). \u0000 null char. Minor. Also: sanitizeText is applied to e.text ?? '' in pushConsole; what if e.text is undefined → '' — untested but trivial. Coverage is decent; completeness of C1 range worth one parametric test (all chars \u0000-\u001f except \t, plus \u007f-\u009f produce no control chars in output). A property-style test: for all codepoints 0-0x9f, output contains no char < 0x20 except none. That would be a strong completeness test. Recommended.

10. Shell scripts vs launchers.test:
    - lib.sh: session_name — tested via lockstep tests (cwd fallback, metachars). Env override and workspace branches tested via JS side mostly; bash side: HERDR_BROWSER_SESSION override path untested directly in bash but trivial. Config-file branch tested JS-side only... The lockstep tests only cover cwd fallback. A bash-side session_name precedence test would be cheap (source lib.sh with various env). Medium value — drift between the two implementations is the real risk, and only one of four precedence branches is lockstep-tested. Note: JS reads config file via `split('\n')[0].replace(/\s/g,'')`; bash `head -n1 | tr -d '[:space:]'`. What about a config file with content only on line 2 (first line empty)? Both treat as empty → fall through. JS: name = '' → falsy → falls to workspace. bash: cfg='' → -n false → falls through. Consistent. What about a config *directory* path that doesn't exist? covered by existsSync/-f.
    - validate_url: tested via open.sh and browse.sh refuse tests: '-rf', 'file:///etc/passwd'. Untested: `http://` with nothing after (the pattern requires `http://?*` — at least one char). E.g. `open.sh http://` → refused. Also `https://` alone. Also uppercase scheme: `HTTP://example.com` — the case pattern in bash is case-sensitive! `http://?*|https://?*` — `HTTP://x` does NOT match → refused (exit 2). But the JS navigate accepts `HTTP://caps.example` (regex has /i flag) and passes it through unchanged. INCONSISTENCY between launcher validate_url and renderer navigate: open.sh refuses `HTTP://example.com` while the pane's address bar accepts it. That's a real cross-implementation drift finding — as testing reviewer I flag that no test pins the intended behavior for uppercase schemes in the shell validator; the JS test explicitly asserts caps acceptance (`HTTP://caps.example`), the shell has no corresponding test, and the shell currently rejects. This is exactly the kind of drift the lockstep tests exist for. High-value finding.
    - Also `http://?*` requires at least one char after scheme — `open.sh "http://"` exits 2; JS: navigate('http://') → explicitScheme true and http → u = 'http://' → new URL throws → rejected. Consistent-ish (both refuse). Good.
    - What about leading whitespace in URL arg? validate_url pattern `http://?*` — ' http://x' → refused. JS navigate trims only in promptInput (value.trim()) — navigate itself doesn't trim; ' http://x' → explicitScheme false → u = 'https:// http://x' → URL parse fail → rejected. Both refuse, fine.
    - open.sh: lock-steal tested, concurrency tested, pretty-print parse tested, missing agent-browser tested, pane-alive focus tested. Untested: `agent-browser open` failure → exit 3 with message. The stub always succeeds. Test: stub agent-browser that exits 1 on `open` → expect status 3, stderr match, and NO pane open (script exits before lock). Medium-high value — exit code contract.
    - open.sh: pane open command failure → exit 4 ('failed to open pane'). Untested — stub herdr that fails on `plugin pane open`. Also pane-id parse failure → warning + rm pidfile but exit 0 (stub returns JSON without pane_id). Untested branch.
    - open.sh: HERDR_PLUGIN_CLICKED_URL fallback when no $1 arg. Untested — that's the actual link-click path (herdr sets clicked url env). The primary production path for link handling is untested! Test: runScript('open.sh', [], env({HERDR_PLUGIN_CLICKED_URL: 'http://localhost:3000'})) → navigates. High value.
    - open.sh: clicked URL that fails validation → exit 2. Also untested.
    - close.sh: tested sweep, order, records removal, report. Untested: HERDR_WORKSPACE_ID unset → sweep skipped (the `[ -n "${HERDR_WORKSPACE_ID:-}" ]` guard); node missing → sweep skipped. Behavior: with no workspace id, close only closes tracked panes. Also close.sh `state_dir` cleanup: shot removal tested. `close_pane` counting — the echo count assertion is `\d+`. The count correctness (closed=2 when two succeed) untested precisely. Weak assertion note: `/closed \d+ pane\(s\)/` would pass even if counting is broken (e.g. always prints 0). Recommend asserting exact count when stub succeeds for known ids: `closed 2 pane(s)`. Also note: herdr stub 'pane close' exit code — stub exits 0 by default at the end (exit 0). With STUB_PANE_ALIVE unset... fine.
    - close.sh sweep node parser: `j.result && j.result.panes` — tested via stub list. Untested: pane list output that's invalid JSON → node prints nothing, no strays, script continues. Also `herdr pane list` failing (exit nonzero) → strays empty. Low.
    - browse.sh: tested URL pass, no-URL, refuses, records ids (duplicate recording asserted 'w9:p7','w9:p7' — that's because stub returns same id; fine). Untested: parse failure warning path (pane opened but id not recorded — silent data loss for close sweep; mitigated by sweep). `herdr pane open` failure → exit 4 untested for browse.sh.
    - browse-pane.sh: completely untested. It's a pane entrypoint: carbonyl missing → prints message + sleep 600 (test would need timeout/stub sleep); URL prompt path (reads stdin); https:// prefixing for bare hosts; zoom from config: `tr -cd '0-9'` strips non-digits — e.g. '125%' → '125'; empty zoom → 100. Testable with stub carbonyl that records args, stdin piped. The prefixing logic (`*) url="https://$url"`) is case-sensitive like validate_url — 'HTTP://x' gets prefixed to 'https://HTTP://x' — broken but edge. Medium value: test bare host → exec carbonyl with https://host; explicit http stays; zoom config parsing ('80\n' → --zoom=80, junk 'abc' → --zoom=100... wait tr -cd '0-9' on 'abc' → '' → zoom='' → ${zoom:-100} → 100. On '12a3' → '123'. Pin these).
    - pane.sh: untested. node missing → message + sleep 600 + exit 1. session export: asserts HERDR_BROWSER_SESSION exported equals session_name output. Testable: stub node that echoes env var. Medium value — this is the glue guaranteeing launcher/renderer session lockstep; a test pinning that pane.sh exports the resolved name guards the "can never derive different names" invariant. Also `exec node bin/renderer.mjs` runs from plugin root.

11. Weak assertions hunt:
    - launchers 'close reports what it did': `/closed \d+ pane\(s\)/` — weak; exact count knowable (one tracked id w9:p7, stub close succeeds → 'closed 1 pane(s)'). But note the sweep also runs: pane-id file removed before sweep... order: tracked loop first (closes w9:p7, closed=1, rm file), then sweep: pane list returns w9:p9 (label Browser) → close_pane w9:p9 → closed=2. So actual output 'closed 2 pane(s)'. Pinning exact count also pins sweep behavior. Assert 'closed 2 pane(s)'. In the test 'close closes pane then session' the count would be 2 as well (p7 + p9). Currently regex allows any number.
    - 'browse records its pane id': asserts duplicate ids ['w9:p7','w9:p7'] — that's an artifact of the stub returning the same id; fine but slightly odd asserting duplicates — it does prove append behavior (>> not >). OK.
    - renderer 'navigate accepts only http(s)': solid assertions (deepEqual opened array, banner match). Good.
    - 'intervalMs clamped': solid.
    - 'sessionExists exact match': solid.
    - pattern.test: assert.ok(pattern.test(url)) — fine; test(url) matches substring, but pattern is anchored (^...$) so fine.
    - renderer.test 'reconcile: rotation evicted entire tail': asserts marker true and newEntries.length === 5 — could strengthen to deepEqual texts ['d'..'h'] but length+marker is nearly equivalent. Minor.
    - launchers 'open with URL': matches log regex — decent.
    - Stub-herdr 'pane read' exits `${STUB_PANE_ALIVE:-1}` — note exit 1 = dead by default; tests that expect 'opens pane' rely on dead default. OK.
    - One weak-ish spot: 'concurrent opens' — `l.match(/plugin pane open/g).length` would throw if null (no match) — actually that failing would be a fine failure mode (TypeError). OK.
    - 'missing agent-browser fails fast': asserts status 1 + stderr hint + no pane open in log. But note log file shared... freshEnv truncates log per run. But `before` writes stubs once; log truncated per freshEnv call. runScript default env = freshEnv() → truncates. Some tests pass custom env built earlier — e.g. concurrency test builds env once for both spawns — log written by both, fine.
    - renderer.test 'deriveSession precedence' uses '/' as cwd for the first three asserts — spawnSync cksum only runs in the 4th branch. Fine.
    - Weak: 'pngDims parses IHDR width/height, rejects junk' — good. Doesn't test buf.length exactly 24 boundary vs 23: Buffer.alloc(10) and alloc(24)-without-IHDR. Length 23 with valid IHDR bytes impossible (needs offset 12..24). Eh.

12. Brittle tests:
    - renderer.test 'mapClickToPage maps clicks...' asserts exact pixel {x:634, y:371} — couples to the half-cell math; that's the actual contract (click accuracy), acceptable and desired. Not brittle in a bad way.
    - 'sessionExists' test relies on stub output JSON shape — fine.
    - launchers tests assert log regexes like /herdr plugin pane open --plugin structupath\.browser/ — couples to argument order in the script; if script reorders flags (still correct behavior), test breaks. Minor brittleness; arg order is somewhat contractual with herdr CLI anyway (flags can reorder safely in CLIs). Acceptable; note lightly.
    - 'pretty-printed pane-open output' pins parser robustness — good, anti-brittle.
    - renderer.test imports Renderer and monkeypatches r.header = () => {} in navigate test — couples to header being called on rejection; fine.
    - Tests construct `new Renderer(...)` which runs fs.mkdirSync + chmodSync on stateDir and spawnSync('sh'...) for chafa — constructor side effects make unit tests environment-dependent (requires sh on PATH, writable tmp). Noted as seam issue.
    - pattern.test depends on Rust-regex↔JS-RegExp subset equivalence — documented in the file. Fine.
    - The lockstep tests run real bash + cksum — environment-dependent (bash, cksum present). Fine on macOS/Linux; CI-portable enough.

13. Missing integration tests (feasible with node:test):
    - Spawn renderer child process with stub agent-browser on PATH, no TTY, HERDR_BROWSER_INTERVAL_MS=250, state dir tmp; assert 'waiting for session' banner appears in stdout; then change stub to list the session and serve get url/title/console + screenshot (write a tiny PNG); assert header shows sanitized URL; send SIGTERM; assert clean exit and shot file removed. Covers run-loop wiring end-to-end (the whole Renderer.run path, tick transitions, header escape output). Seams needed: none really — env vars + PATH suffice; the renderer never exits on its own so test must kill it (use timeout + SIGTERM). One complication: probeKitty checks process.stdin.isTTY — in spawn with stdio pipes it's not TTY → resolves '' immediately. mode = pickRenderMode(...) → chafa probe via PATH (exclude chafa → 'text' mode; avoids needing chafa). Screenshot still runs in text mode? tick calls browser.screenshot(tmp) regardless of mode — yes, then renderImage returns early in text mode. So stub agent-browser must handle 'screenshot <file>' by writing a file (renderer reads it for md5 — readFileSync(tmp) throws if missing → failure path). Stub must write a real file. Feasible: stub bash writes PNG header bytes.
    - Passive invariant test: stub agent-browser logging all invocations; run renderer 1s with session never existing; assert log contains only 'session list' calls, never 'get'/'console'/'screenshot'. This is THE core safety property. High value.
    - navigate → quit ownership: covered at unit level for selfCreated flag; cleanup's actual `agent-browser close` spawn untested — integration: spawn renderer, stub says session missing, feed... no TTY so no input. Unit-test cleanup by stubbing PATH and calling r.cleanup() with selfCreated true; assert stub log has close; with selfCreated false assert no close. process.exit not called by cleanup (only by signal handlers/onInput q) — cleanup itself doesn't exit. Good, directly callable. But cleanup writes escapes to stdout and calls process.stdin.isTTY — fine in tests.
    - clickAt unit tests with fake browser + fake size(): set r.mode, monkeypatch r.size = () => ({cols:100, imageRows:30, imageTopRow:3,...}), write a fake PNG (24-byte IHDR header enough for pngDims — readFileSync then pngDims reads first 24 bytes; need only header) into r.shot; fake browser.eval capturing js; assert click at (50,17) produces elementFromPoint(634, 371); click at console row (e.g. row 40) → no eval; click above header rows → no eval; missing shot → no eval; corrupt shot → no eval. High value — this is the click-mapping correctness anchor end-to-end from SGR coords to JS eval. Seams: size() reads this.mode and process.stdout.columns/rows — monkeypatching size() is easiest, or set process.stdout.columns in test (mutable). Actually size() computes from process.stdout.columns || 80 — in node:test, stdout may be a pipe → columns undefined → 80/24 deterministic! So mode + default 80x24 gives deterministic geometry without monkeypatching: rows=24, mode 'symbols' → consoleRows = max(4, 7) = 7, imageRows = 24-7-4 = 13, imageTopRow 3. Deterministic unless run in a TTY... node --test typically pipes. Slight env-dependence risk; setting process.stdout.columns/rows explicitly in the test is safer. Note as recommendation.

14. Production seams needed:
    - Renderer constructor side effects (mkdir, chmod, cksum spawn, chafa probe) — acceptable already since tests pass HERDR_PLUGIN_STATE_DIR; but the chafa probe and cwd-cksum spawn make construction slow-ish (~10ms each). Fine.
    - `cleanup()` hardcodes 'agent-browser' binary (not this.browser's bin / no injection) — to unit-test the selfCreated close without PATH manipulation, a seam (e.g. this.bin) would help. Currently makeBrowser takes bin but cleanup doesn't use it. Recommend either PATH-based test or a small seam. Also note inconsistency: makeBrowser default bin 'agent-browser'; cleanup hardcodes same string — drift risk if renamed.
    - `run()` infinite loop — for integration test, child-process spawn is the seam (no code change needed). For unit-testing the loop backoff (idleTicks increments when sig unchanged), no seam exists — sig()/idleTicks testable indirectly: call r.sig() before/after fake ticks. Actually the backoff loop itself (idleTicks++ on no-change, reset on change) lives inline in run() — untestable without running run(). A seam: extract the "update idleTicks" decision or make the loop body injectable. Alternatively test via child process with HB_DEBUG... no. Recommend extracting a small method `async step()` containing sig-compare + tick + idle update, so tests can drive steps with a fake browser and assert idleTicks progression. Medium value.
    - `onInput` 'q'/Ctrl-C calls process.exit — test needs monkeypatch of process.exit (works: temporarily replace) or child-process. Also cleanup inside. Feasible with monkeypatch; note process.exit replacement is a common node:test pattern.
    - tick() uses fs.readFileSync(tmp) after screenshot — fake browser must actually write the file (fs.writeFileSync in fake). Feasible without seams.

15. Also check: `pushConsole` prefix uses e.type 'error' → ✖, 'warn'/'warning' → ⚠. Test recommendations: prefix mapping + 500 cap + sanitize-in-display but raw-in-reconcile invariant (reconcile matching needs raw texts — the consoleState stores consoleTail(entries) raw; pushConsole sanitizes display copy. A test asserting consoleState.tail holds RAW text (with escapes) while consoleLines holds sanitized would pin this deliberate design. Currently zero coverage of pushConsole.)

16. `renderConsole` truncation/slicing and divider — untested; testable by capturing process.stdout.write calls (monkeypatch). Assert: writes divider at rows-consoleRows, shows last consoleRows-1 lines, truncates long lines to cols. Medium value.

17. `header()` — banner precedence over url, about:blank hint, inverse video escapes. Capture writes; assert line1 contains session & mode; banner line replaces url line. Medium.

18. promptInput tests: submit on Enter with trimmed value; empty submit does nothing (no onSubmit); Esc cancels; backspace; chunk with multiple chars; ignores control chars. High-ish value (address bar is a core feature; regressions here are user-visible). Also openPrompt renderBottom. All feasible with monkeypatched renderBottom/process.stdout.write.

19. userAction: resets idleTicks, enqueues action then a tick; action throwing is swallowed. Test with fake browser that throws → assert no unhandled rejection and tick called. The paintQueue error-tolerance (`enqueue(fn)` with `then(fn, () => {})`) — a throwing fn must not poison the queue. Testable: enqueue(() => { throw }) then enqueue(() => flag = true) → assert flag set. Good cheap test.

20. onInput gating when unattached: 'b' should NOT call browser.back when unattached; 'u' allowed. Attached: all keys work. Mouse click swallowed when unattached. High value — driving a session the pane doesn't own is a core ownership concern... wait, actually the gate is the opposite: when unattached (no session), b/f/r/j/k are ignored. When attached (agent-owned session), keys DO drive the shared session — intended ("User-initiated drive of the shared session"). So test pins the gate.

21. One more code-level observation for the audit: in tick(), `this.lastUrl = sanitizeText(url)` — url from browser.url() could throw if session died mid-tick → failure path. Fine. `consoleState` update happens even when reconcile returns marker — good.

22. In run(): `process.stdout.on('resize')` debounce 150ms → enqueue(redrawAll) — untested, hard to test without TTY; skip or note.

23. deriveSession: HERDR_BROWSER_SESSION env takes precedence — but note pane.sh ALWAYS sets HERDR_BROWSER_SESSION from session_name, so within the pane the env branch always wins; consistent.

24. The red-team artifact exists from a previous review — I shouldn't just copy it; my analysis above is independent and complete. I could skim it to cross-check I didn't miss anything big. Let me skim the prior testing-reviewer input artifact... Actually the artifacts are inputs, and red-team output exists. A quick skim of red-team output might reveal known issues (like uppercase scheme inconsistency) which would corroborate. Let me check quickly.

Let me also verify the uppercase-scheme claim about validate_url in bash. `case "$1" in http://?*|https://?*)` — bash case is case-sensitive by default (nocasematch off). So `HTTP://example.com` does not match → return 1 → refused exit 2. And the JS navigate test asserts `HTTP://caps.example` IS accepted and passed through unchanged as 'HTTP://caps.example'. So yes: launcher refuses what the renderer accepts. Real drift, and no test pins intended shell behavior. Also browse-pane.sh prefixing: `case "$url" in http://*|https://*) ;; *) url="https://$url" ;;` — 'HTTP://x' → prefixed to 'https://HTTP://x' → carbonyl gets garbage. Same class of issue.

Also open.sh: `agent-browser open` failure → exit 3 — but the message says "failed to open $url". Note the AGENT_BROWSER_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS export is tested? No — no test asserts the env var is passed to agent-browser. Could assert stub logs env. Minor.

Also note: open.sh with URL — the stub agent-browser echoes `agent-browser $@` — log shows `--session herdr-ws-w9 open http://localhost:3000`. Good.

close.sh sweep requires `command -v node` — in freshEnv PATH includes dirname(process.execPath) so node available. The 'sweep' tests rely on it.

One more thing to verify — launchers.test 'close removes only this workspace screenshot cache': close.sh also runs pane close etc. fine.

Let me quickly skim the red-team output artifact for corroboration (optional). And check README for intended behavior statements (e.g., ownership rules) to anchor severity.

Actually, time to be efficient. I have enough for a thorough report. Let me do a quick sanity check on a couple of factual claims I'll make:

Claim A: bash `case` is case-sensitive → validate_url rejects 'HTTP://x'. Verify quickly.
Claim B: browse-pane.sh prefixes 'HTTP://x' → 'https://HTTP://x'.
Claim C: onInput multi-char chunk 'jj' hits no case (switch on whole string) → dropped. Verify by reading code: `switch (s)` where s is the whole chunk string — yes, 'jj' matches no case. And parseSgrMouse only first match per chunk. These are production behavior notes.
Claim D: tick() passive branch makes no get/console/screenshot calls — verify code: yes, returns after header() when session doesn't exist.
Claim E: cleanup hardcodes 'agent-browser' binary name — yes, spawnSync('agent-browser', ...) at line ~465.
Claim F: reconcileConsole with duplicate tail texts — suffix search finds latest occurrence; potential to misalign with duplicate log lines. E.g., prev tail ['x','x'], buffer ['x','x','x','new'] with count saturated... Below ring: head alignment handles. Saturated: winLen 2, search from end: texts ['x','x','x','new'], end starts at 3 ('new' vs 'x' no), end=2: texts[1..2]='x','x' match → newEntries = ['x','new'] — but true new entries were ['x','new']? If prev buffer was [.., 'x','x'] and new buffer is ['x','x','x','new'] after rotation... hard to construct a definitively wrong case without knowing true history; the heuristic is best-effort. A test pinning duplicate-window behavior (whatever it currently does) is still valuable as a regression anchor. I'll frame it as: untested ambiguous-alignment behavior with repeated identical lines (common with pages logging in loops).

Let me verify claims A/B quickly with bash, and check README ownership docs.

text:
# herdr-browser

A driveable browser pane for [Herdr](https://herdr.dev). Open a URL from an
action or a Ctrl+click on a localhost link, and get a live view of a headless
Chrome session inside a Herdr pane: screenshot, URL/title, and streaming
console output. Screenshots render as real pixels via the Kitty graphics
protocol, with an ANSI fallback everywhere else.

Built for the agent workflow: when a coding agent drives
[agent-browser](https://github.com/vercel-labs/agent-browser) in one pane, this
plugin gives you a live human-visible view of the same browser session in
another — without touching the agent's page state.

## Install

```sh
herdr plugin install StructuPath/herdr-browser
```

### Prerequisites

- **agent-browser** — the browser engine (daemon-backed headless Chrome):

  ```sh
  npm install -g agent-browser && agent-browser install
  ```

- **chafa** — renders screenshots into the terminal (optional but strongly
  recommended; without it the pane runs text-only):

  ```sh
  brew install chafa        # macOS
  ```

- **Node.js >= 20** on your PATH (the pane renderer).

Tested against herdr 0.7.4 and agent-browser 0.28.x.

## Usage

The plugin ships two actions (Herdr plugins cannot ship default keybindings —
add your own):

```toml
# ~/.config/herdr/config.toml
[[keys.command]]
key = "prefix+b"
type = "plugin_action"
command = "structupath.browser.open"
description = "browser pane"
```

- **Open** (`structupath.browser.open`) — with no URL (the keybinding/invoke
  path), attaches the pane **view-only** to this workspace's browser session
  without navigating it. With a URL (the link-click path), navigates first.
- **Close** (`structupath.browser.close`) — closes the pane and ends this
  workspace's browser session.
- **Localhost links** — a modified click (Cmd/Ctrl+click) on any
  `http://localhost:PORT`, `127.0.0.1`, or `[::1]` URL printed in a Herdr pane
  opens it in the browser pane instead of your system browser. Plain clicks
  keep their default behavior.
- **Browse** (`structupath.browser.browse`) — an *interactive* browser pane
  via [carbonyl](https://github.com/fathyb/carbonyl) (Chromium rendered into

content.text:
# herdr-browser

A driveable browser pane for [Herdr](https://herdr.dev). Open a URL from an
action or a Ctrl+click on a localhost link, and get a live view of a headless
Chrome session inside a Herdr pane: screenshot, URL/title, and streaming
console output. Screenshots render as real pixels via the Kitty graphics
protocol, with an ANSI fallback everywhere else.

Built for the agent workflow: when a coding agent drives
[agent-browser](https://github.com/vercel-labs/agent-browser) in one pane, this
plugin gives you a live human-visible view of the same browser session in
another — without touching the agent's page state.

## Install

```sh
herdr plugin install StructuPath/herdr-browser
```

### Prerequisites

- **agent-browser** — the browser engine (daemon-backed headless Chrome):

  ```sh
  npm install -g agent-browser && agent-browser install
  ```

- **chafa** — renders screenshots into the terminal (optional but strongly
  recommended; without it the pane runs text-only):

  ```sh
  brew install chafa        # macOS
  ```

- **Node.js >= 20** on your PATH (the pane renderer).

Tested against herdr 0.7.4 and agent-browser 0.28.x.

## Usage

The plugin ships two actions (Herdr plugins cannot ship default keybindings —
add your own):

```toml
# ~/.config/herdr/config.toml
[[keys.command]]
key = "prefix+b"
type = "plugin_action"
command = "structupath.browser.open"
description = "browser pane"
```

- **Open** (`structupath.browser.open`) — with no URL (the keybinding/invoke
  path), attaches the pane **view-only** to this workspace's browser session
  without navigating it. With a URL (the link-click path), navigates first.
- **Close** (`structupath.browser.close`) — closes the pane and ends this
  workspace's browser session.
- **Localhost links** — a modified click (Cmd/Ctrl+click) on any
  `http://localhost:PORT`, `127.0.0.1`, or `[::1]` URL printed in a Herdr pane
  opens it in the browser pane instead of your system browser. Plain clicks
  keep their default behavior.
- **Browse** (`structupath.browser.browse`) — an *interactive* browser pane
  via [carbonyl](https://github.com/fathyb/carbonyl) (Chromium rendered into
  the terminal: mouse, scrolling, typing all work). With no URL it prompts —
  an address bar in a pane. Requires `npm install -g carbonyl`. This is a
  personal browser with its own Chromium; it does not share the agent's
  session — use the viewer pane (Open) for that.

## Viewer controls

The viewer pane is passive until you act; every action below drives the
shared session directly (your agent sees the same browser state):

| Input | Action |
|-------|--------|
| `u` | address bar — type any URL, Enter to go (`https://` assumed) |
| click on the screenshot | clicks the real page at that spot |
| `i` | type text into the focused element |
| `b` / `f` | history back / forward |
| `r` | reload |

| `j` / `k` / space | scroll down / up |
| `q` | close the pane (session keeps running) |

Clicks map through the screenshot geometry to page coordinates, so what you
click is what Chrome clicks. Expect roughly a one-second round trip per
interaction — this is browsing-by-snapshot, built for dev verification, not
for reading Twitter.

## Real-pixel screenshots (optional)

By default screenshots render as ANSI half-blocks. For real pixels, enable
Herdr's experimental Kitty graphics support (requires a Kitty-graphics-capable
outer terminal — Ghostty, Kitty, WezTerm):

```toml
# ~/.config/herdr/config.toml
[experimental]
kitty_graphics = true
```

Then `herdr server reload-config`. The renderer probes for support at pane
start and picks the best mode automatically; override with the `render` config
(see below) or `HERDR_BROWSER_RENDER=kitty|symbols|text`.

## The session model

Each Herdr workspace gets its own browser session, named `herdr-ws-<id>`. The
pane is a **passive viewer**: it never navigates, never clears the console,
and never kills the session — closing the pane leaves the browser running for
whatever agent is using it. Cleanup is the Close action, or agent-browser's
idle timeout (`AGENT_BROWSER_IDLE_TIMEOUT_MS`, disabled by default).

To watch the session your coding agent is actually using, either tell the
agent to use the workspace session:

> run agent-browser with `--session herdr-ws-<id>` (the pane header shows the
> exact name)

or point the pane at the agent's session by writing the session name to the
plugin config:

```sh
echo "my-agents-session" > "$(herdr plugin config-dir structupath.browser)/session"
```

Config files (one value per line, in the plugin config dir):

| File      | Values                    | Meaning                          |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|
| `session` | any agent-browser session | watch this session instead       |
| `render`  | `kitty` `symbols` `text`  | force a render mode              |

## Privacy note

The pane caches the latest screenshot (and nothing else) as a PNG in Herdr's
plugin state dir, `chmod 600`, deleted when the pane closes. If your agent
browses authenticated pages, that content is visible in the pane and briefly
on disk — treat screen shares accordingly.

## Troubleshooting

- **Pane says "session … is not running"** — nothing has started that
  workspace's browser yet. Invoke Open with a URL, click a localhost link, or
  have your agent use the session name in the header.
- **Text-only mode** — chafa isn't installed (`brew install chafa`).
- **No image, garbled block art** — your outer terminal doesn't support Kitty
  graphics; the renderer should fall back to `symbols` automatically. Force it:
  `echo symbols > "$(herdr plugin config-dir structupath.browser)/render"`.
- **Plugin logs** — `herdr plugin log list --plugin structupath.browser`.

## Development

```sh
git clone https://github.com/StructuPath/herdr-browser
herdr plugin link ./herdr-browser
npm test
```

MIT © StructuPath

F1 (High-value test gap, confidence 85): tick()'s passive invariant untested — the core safety property "renderer never auto-creates a session" (bin/renderer.mjs:290-299). When unattached and session missing, tick must call ONLY sessionExists — never url/title/console/screenshot. Zero tests cover tick(). A regression here (e.g. reordering the guard) spawns a headless Chrome per workspace pane — the exact behavior the code comments say must never happen. Feasible today with a fake browser recording calls (no seam needed) or via child-process integration with a logging stub binary.

F2 (High-value test gap, confidence 85): clickAt (bin/renderer.mjs:475-487) untested — SGR→page coordinate pipeline end-to-end. mapClickToPage is unit-tested in isolation but clickAt's guards (click outside image rows, missing/corrupt screenshot, eval JS construction with elementFromPoint(x,y)) have no coverage. A geometry regression in size() interplay or the row guards silently mis-clicks the real page. Feasible: fake browser.eval capture, write 24-byte PNG header to r.shot, set process.stdout.columns/rows or monkeypatch size().

F3 (High-value test gap, confidence 80): tick() failure/degradation paths untested (bin/renderer.mjs:328-343): failures 1-2 keep state, >=3 with session alive → 'not responding' banner, >=3 with session gone → detach + 'session ended' + failures reset; success resets failures. Also screenshot-hash paths: unchanged hash deletes tmp and skips renderImage; changed hash renames + chmod 600. None covered.

F4 (Real behavior gap found while auditing, confidence 90): navigate() accepts 'mailto:user@example.com' and opens it as 'https://mailto:user@example.com/' — the inline comment at bin/renderer.mjs:436-439 claims schemes without // "end up as a non-numeric port and fail parsing", which is false when the input contains '@' (parses as userinfo@host). F5 (Cross-implementation drift, confidence 95): validate_url in scripts/lib.sh:41-47 is case-sensitive (bash case) → refuses 'HTTP://example.com' with exit 2; renderer navigate() accepts 'HTTP://caps.example' and the JS test explicitly asserts that acceptance (tests/renderer.test.mjs:150-ish). browse-pane.sh:20-23 has the same case-sensitivity and would mangle to 'https://HTTP://example.com'. Verified by execution. No test pins uppercase-scheme behavior in shell; the lockstep testing approach (used for session_name) doesn't extend to URL policy. Either behavior may be intended, but they can't both be "the same policy" as the comments claim.

F6 (High-value test gap, confidence 80): open.sh's HERDR_PLUGIN_CLICKED_URL fallback (scripts/open.sh:14) — the primary link-click production path — untested. All open.sh tests pass URL as $1. Also untested: clicked-URL failing validation → exit 2.

F7 (Medium, confidence 75): open.sh error exits untested: agent-browser open failure → exit 3 (scripts/open.sh:27-30); herdr pane open failure → exit 4 (line 58-61); pane-id parse failure → warning + pidfile removal + exit 0 (lines 63-68). Stub always succeeds on these today.

F8 (Medium, confidence 75): promptInput (bin/renderer.mjs:415-430) untested: Enter submits trimmed value; empty value does NOT submit; Esc cancels without submit; backspace; multi-char chunks; control chars ignored. Core address-bar UX.

F9 (Medium, confidence 75): pushConsole (bin/renderer.mjs:276-287) untested: error/warn prefix mapping, sanitize-for-display-but-raw-for-reconcile invariant (consoleState.tail raw vs consoleLines sanitized), 500-line cap + consolePushes monotonicity (which sig()/backoff depends on).

F10 (Medium, confidence 70): onInput gating (bin/renderer.mjs:388-407) untested: unattached pane ignores b/f/r/i/j/k and mouse clicks (only u/q/Ctrl-C live); attached pane drives. Also pins the 'q' → cleanup path.

F11 (Low/observation, confidence 60): onInput processes at most one key per chunk (switch on whole string, bin/renderer.mjs:398) and parseSgrMouse captures only the first SGR report per chunk (bin/renderer.mjs:106) — batched terminal input silently drops events; promptInput does loop per-char (line 417), so the asymmetry looks unintentional. No test pins either behavior. Production observation + missing tests.

F12 (Medium, confidence 75): cleanup() ownership behavior untested (bin/renderer.mjs:495-510): selfCreated=true → spawns `agent-browser --session X close`; false → never. Also deletes shot + tmp. The binary name is hardcoded here (not the makeBrowser bin) — a drift seam. Testable via PATH stub; a `this.bin` seam would make it cleaner.

F13 (Low-medium, confidence 70): reconcileConsole untested edges: empty prev.tail with count>0 (vacuous alignment vs saturated marker-full-set), boundary prev.count === ringSize with no rotation, duplicate/repeated tail texts (pages logging identical lines in loops) where suffix search finds latest occurrence — behavior unpinned.

F16 (Brittleness, low, confidence 65): launchers tests regex-match the stub log for exact flag order (e.g. /herdr plugin pane open --plugin structupath\.browser/) — a harmless flag reorder in the script breaks tests without behavior change. Acceptable given herdr CLI contract; noted only.

F17 (Medium gap, confidence 70): browse-pane.sh and pane.sh have zero coverage. browse-pane.sh: bare-host https:// prefixing (case-sensitive, mangles HTTP://), zoom config parsing `tr -cd '0-9'` ('12a3'→'123', 'abc'→'' → 100), empty URL prompt exit. pane.sh: exports HERDR_BROWSER_SESSION from session_name (the lockstep glue) — testable with stub node/carbonyl recording argv/env. []; title() null → ''. sessionExists catch → false (stub exiting nonzero/garbage). All feasible with stub binaries — same pattern as existing sessionExists test.
